properties.name: Bijoliya Kisan Andolan (1897–1941)
properties.leaders: Sitaram Das, Vijay Singh Pathik, Manikya Lal Verma
properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + standard freedom-struggle historiography

facts: India's longest-running organised peasant movement — 44 years against the 84-plus 'lag-bag' cesses levied by the Bijoliya thikana under Mewar.
Sadhu Sitaram Das started the agitation in 1897; Vijay Singh Pathik built it into an all-India cause after 1916, drawing Tilak's and Gandhi's endorsement.
The 1922 Bijoliya Settlement — the first peasant-victory settlement in princely India — reduced 84 taxes to 30 and became a template for later kisan movements.
The 1941 final resolution ended the last cesses and returned confiscated lands — one of pre-Independence India's few complete peasant victories.
The Dhakad caste dominated the movement — the first princely-state peasant struggle led by a single farming caste in India.
When "Pratap" newspaper carried Bijoliya's atrocities in print (c. 1919), the movement became a national cause with Tilak and Gandhi backing it.
The struggle produced Manikya Lal Verma, later the founder of Mewar Praja Mandal (1938).

properties.name: Begun Kisan Andolan (1921–23)
properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + standard freedom-struggle historiography

facts: Sister peasant movement to Bijoliya — Begun thikana peasants demanded abolition of the same 'lag-bag' cesses.
Ramnarayan Chaudhary and Vijay Singh Pathik led the agitation; 1923 saw peasants shot at Govind Pura by state forces.
The 1923 Rajputana Political Officer's inquiry sided with the peasants; this became a rare instance of the British colonial government intervening against a princely jagirdar.
A break-away wing of the Bijoliya movement — Begun's cesses (lag-bag) fell heaviest on Dhakad and Gujar tenants.
Ramnarayan Chaudhary took over after Vijay Singh Pathik was jailed; the movement continued as a non-violent tenants-' strike.

properties.name: Ekki (Bhil) Movement (1921–22)
properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + standard freedom-struggle historiography

facts: Motilal Tejawat's tribal-solidarity movement across Mewar-Vagad-Sirohi Bhils — 'ekki' = 'unity' — against forced labour and thikana cesses.
The 7 March 1922 Neemuchana-style massacre at Pal-Chitariya (Sirohi) — Mewar Bhil Corps killed ~1,200 Bhil protestors — is Rajasthan's Jallianwala Bagh.
Tejawat's khadi-clad tribal army carried tricolour flags 5 years before the AICC officially adopted it as a national symbol.
Ran under Motilal Tejavat, an Oswal from Udaipur — the first non-tribal to organise Bhils on caste-blind demands.
At Nimda village (Vijayanagar state), March 1922, the combined armies of Mewar-Sirohi-Idar-Vijayanagar opened fire on the Bhil conference — an estimated 1,200 killed.
Tejavat went underground for eight years; surrendered in 1929 on Gandhi's advice.

properties.name: Neemuchana Massacre (14 May 1925)
properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + standard freedom-struggle historiography

facts: Alwar state forces opened fire on a peasant assembly at Neemuchana on 14 May 1925 — killed ~200 peasants protesting land-revenue hikes.
Called 'Alwar's Jallianwala Bagh' — Mahatma Gandhi condemned the killings in Young India (June 1925) as a 'crime of tyranny'.
The massacre triggered the formation of the Alwar Rajya Praja Mandal (1938) and long-term pressure on Alwar Maharaja Jai Singh.
Alwar peasants had refused a new 40 % land-revenue hike; 500-600 gathered at Neemuchana on 14 May 1925.
Colonel Ramsay's troops opened fire — 156 killed, 600 wounded — often called the "Jallianwala Bagh of Rajputana".
Gandhi wrote in Young India that "the Alwar state carnage matches Punjab in cruelty".

properties.name: Sikar Kisan Andolan / Shekhawati (1922–34)
properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + standard freedom-struggle historiography

facts: Shekhawati's Jat peasant movement against Rajput jagirdars — one of pre-Independence India's largest caste-and-class agitations.
Chaudhary Ghasiram Verma organised the peasants; the 1934 Kumbharam Arya settlement won revenue reforms across the Shekhawati thikanas.
Formative movement — many Shekhawati political leaders (including future PM Chaudhary Charan Singh's mentor circle) trained in this agitation.
Shekhawati Jat peasants organised against the Rao Raja of Sikar (a Thikana under Jaipur); leader Chetram Jat, later Pt. Tara Kesh.
Ran alongside the Kisan Sabha of Sardar Harlal Singh — culminated in the abolition of Jagir cesses after 1947.

properties.name: Mewar Praja Mandal (Udaipur, 24 April 1938)
properties.leaders: Manikya Lal Verma, Balwant Singh Mehta, Bhurelal Baya
properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + Rajasthan freedom-struggle records

facts: Founded 24 April 1938 by Manikya Lal Verma at Udaipur — Mewar's principal Congress-aligned platform for civil-liberties and responsible-government demands.
Verma spent nearly a decade in Mewar prisons for organising it; his letters from jail became foundational anti-monarchic literature.
Merged into the Indian National Congress in 1948 when Mewar joined the United State of Rajasthan (Integration Phase 3).
Founded by Manikya Lal Verma, a graduate of the Bijoliya movement — its aim was responsible government, not just tax relief.
Nathdwara, Bhilwara and Chittorgarh were the movement's second-tier centres during the Quit India struggle.
The Maharana banned the organisation immediately after its formation; workers went underground and re-surfaced in 1942.

properties.name: Marwar Praja Mandal (Jodhpur, 16 May 1938)
properties.leaders: Jai Narayan Vyas, Ranchhod Das Gattani, Bhanwar Lal Sarraf
properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + Rajasthan freedom-struggle records

facts: Founded 16 May 1938 at Jodhpur by Jai Narayan Vyas — Marwar's biggest civil-disobedience platform against the Rathore autocracy.
Jai Narayan Vyas served two long jail terms; became Rajasthan's third Chief Minister (1951-52 and 1952-54).
The 1942 Marwar Lok Parishad session at Jodhpur — 30,000 attendees — was the largest political rally in a princely state during Quit India.
Founded 16 May 1938 in Jodhpur under Jainarayan Vyas, with Bhanwarlal Sarraf and Prayagram Bhandari as merchant patrons.
The 1942 movement here produced its own martyr — Balmukund Bissa, who died on hunger-strike in Jodhpur jail.
About 400 Marwaris went to jail during Quit India — the largest turnout from any princely state.

properties.name: Jaipur Rajya Praja Mandal (1936)
properties.leaders: Kapoor Chand Patni, Jamnalal Bajaj, Hiralal Shastri, Chiranji Lal Mishra
properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + Rajasthan freedom-struggle records

facts: Founded 1936 by Kapoor Chand Patni and Jamnalal Bajaj — the earliest Praja Mandal in the big Rajputana states.
Hiralal Shastri became its most influential leader — he was Rajasthan's first Chief Minister (April-Dec 1949) after Integration.
Coordinated with Jaipur's textile-mill workers' union — one of few Praja Mandals with a strong industrial-worker base.
Established 1936 under Jamnalal Bajaj; later led by Pt. Hiralal Shastri who negotiated a "responsible-government" settlement with PM Mirza Ismail.
The Quit India split — Baba Harishchand set up the parallel "Azad Morcha" (1942) when Shastri refused to launch the movement.
Post-integration (1949), Shastri became Rajasthan's first Chief Minister.

properties.name: Bikaner Rajya Praja Mandal (1936, Kolkata)
properties.founded: 22 July 1936 (founded in Kolkata due to ban in Bikaner)
properties.leaders: Magha Ram Vaidya, Lakshman Das Swami, Raghuvardayal Goyal
properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + Rajasthan freedom-struggle records

facts: Unusually, founded outside its own state — 22 July 1936 in Kolkata, because Ganga Singh's Bikaner state had banned political organising.
Magha Ram Vaidya smuggled Praja Mandal literature into Bikaner via the Bikaner-Kolkata railway route.
Legalised inside Bikaner only in 1942 when Maharaja Sadul Singh relaxed the ban.
Founded outside the state (Kolkata, 1936) — Ganga Singh's Bikaner exiled its leader Raghuvar Dayal Goyal from the moment the Mandal formed.
Nemichand Aanchaliya was sentenced to 7 years rigorous imprisonment for a critical article — the harshest single sentence during Quit India in Rajasthan.
Flag Satyagraha (hoisting the tricolour) was the Mandal's signature tactic; the Bikaner darbar treated it as sedition.

properties.name: Alwar Rajya Praja Mandal (1938)
properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + Rajasthan freedom-struggle records

facts: Founded 1938 by Kunwar Madho Singh — the direct political response to the 1925 Neemuchana Massacre and the state's authoritarian regime.
Merged Alwar into the Matsya Union (Integration Phase 1) on 18 March 1948; Kunwar Madho Singh joined the Matsya Union cabinet.
Its 1946 satyagraha at Alwar Bala Qila secured the reinstatement of exiled Praja Mandal leaders — a pre-Integration political victory.
Formed after Maharaja Jai Singh — an early progressive who banned child marriage in 1903 — was himself exiled by the British.
Diwan Faizullah Khan's sympathy with 1857 rebels foreshadowed the state's later constitutional agitation.

properties.name: Kota Rajya Praja Mandal (1939)
properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + Rajasthan freedom-struggle records

facts: Founded 1939 in Kota by Nayanuram Sharma — the Hadoti belt's principal civil-liberties platform.
Coordinated with the Bundi and Jhalawar branches to form the Hadoti States People's Federation.
Merged with the USR-Congress on 25 March 1948 (Integration Phase 2) when Kota joined the United State of Rajasthan.
Founded 1939 by Pt. Nayanuram Sharma and Pt. Abhinn Hari; its first session at Mangrol (Baran) chose responsible government as the goal.
Quit India high point: Kota students locked the state police in barracks and hoisted the tricolour on Rampura Kotwali (14-16 Aug 1942) for two weeks.

properties.name: Bharatpur Rajya Praja Mandal (1938)
properties.leaders: Kishan Lal Joshi, Gopilal Yadav, Master Adityendra
properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + Rajasthan freedom-struggle records

facts: Founded 1938 in Bharatpur — the Matsya region's largest civil-liberties movement outside Alwar.
Kishan Lal Joshi coordinated with the Jat-community Kisan movements and the Delhi-facing satyagraha network.
Bharatpur joined the Matsya Union on 18 March 1948 (Integration Phase 1) with the Praja Mandal leadership integrated into the Union cabinet.
Grew out of the Hindi Sahitya Samiti (1912) — the first Praja Mandal in Rajasthan with a clear literary-cultural base.
Maharaja Kishan Singh was forced by the British to leave the throne for his progressive stance — his successor banned meetings and even possession of national leaders' pictures.
The 1942 movement here ended in the state's promise of an elected-majority assembly after floods forced a pause and negotiations.

properties.name: Samp Sabha & Mangarh Massacre (1913)
properties.source: Rajasthan Adhyan Class 11 Ch 1 (Public Awakening & Freedom Struggle) + standard historiography

facts: Govind Guru founded Samp Sabha in 1905 to reform Bhil society — end drinking, forced labour and cesses; educate the young; settle disputes in tribal panchayats.
The annual Ashwin Purnima gathering at Mangarh Hill (Rajasthan-Gujarat border) grew into a mass movement of Bhil-Garasia farmers.

On 17 Nov 1913 the British-Mewar-Vagad forces surrounded the hill and opened fire — an estimated 1,500 tribals were killed; Govind Guru got the death sentence, commuted to 20 years, released after 10.
The site is India's only tribal-massacre memorial anchored to a hilltop; the National Mangarh Dham Memorial was formally dedicated by the PM in Nov 2022.

properties.name: Motilal Tejavat — Bhil Ekki Movement (1921-29)
properties.source: Rajasthan Adhyan Class 11 Ch 1 (Public Awakening & Freedom Struggle) + standard historiography

facts: Tejavat, an Oswal from Udaipur, resigned Thikana service in 1921 and organised Bhils against forced labour (baith-begar) and Jagirdar cesses (lag-bag).
Nimda (Vijayanagar state) massacre, Mar 1922 — armies of Mewar, Sirohi, Idar and Vijayanagar surrounded a Bhil conference and opened fire, killing about 1,200.
Tejavat went underground for 8 years; surrendered at Idar in 1929 on Gandhi's advice; released 1936.
The Ekki movement produced the second wave of tribal awakening in Rajasthan after Govind Guru's Samp Sabha.

properties.name: Sagarmal Gopa — Jaisalmer Gundaraj (1946)
properties.source: Rajasthan Adhyan Class 11 Ch 1 (Public Awakening & Freedom Struggle) + standard historiography

facts: Sagarmal Gopa printed "Jaisalmer Mein Gundaraj" (1940) exposing Maharawal Jawahar Singh's misrule — was exiled immediately.
On his return in 1941 for his father's funeral, arrested and given 6 years rigorous imprisonment for sedition; Thanedar Guman Singh reportedly tortured him.
On 3 April 1946 Gopa was burned alive by pouring kerosene on him inside the jail — died 4 April; the news spread nationally with Nehru and Jainarayan Vyas condemning it.
Rajasthan's most enduring symbol of Prajamandal-era judicial murder; Gopa Jayanti is observed 3 April in Jaisalmer every year.

properties.name: Balmukund Bissa — Jodhpur Prajamandal Martyr (1942)
properties.source: Rajasthan Adhyan Class 11 Ch 1 (Public Awakening & Freedom Struggle) + standard historiography

facts: A Marwari from Pilwa (Didwana) who opened Jawahar Khadi Bhandar in Jodhpur (1934); made it the nerve-centre of Marwar Praja Mandal activity.
Arrested during the Quit India movement (1942); died in Jodhpur jail after a prolonged hunger-strike and reported torture — the first Praja Mandal hunger-strike martyr in Rajasthan.
His martyrdom galvanised the ~400 Marwaris who filled Jodhpur jails during Quit India — the largest turnout from any princely state.

properties.name: Barhath Revolutionary Family — Shahpura
properties.leaders: Kesari Singh Barhath (1872-1941), Pratap Singh Barhath, Zoravar Singh Barhath
properties.source: Rajasthan Adhyan Class 11 Ch 1 (Public Awakening & Freedom Struggle) + standard historiography

facts: Progenitor family of Rajasthan's revolutionary underground; the three-generation Charan household of Shahpura sent all its men to jail.
Kesari Singh's "Chetavani Ka Chungatya" verses (1903) persuaded Mewar Maharana Fateh Singh not to attend the Delhi Durbar.
Pratap Singh was tortured to death in Bareilly Jail (May 1918) after a mahant-murder charge to raise revolutionary funds.
Kesari Singh got 20 years in Hazaribagh, released after 16; Zoravar Singh was involved in the 1912 Delhi bomb attempt on Viceroy Hardinge.

properties.name: Arjun Lal Sethi — Vardhman Pathshala, Jaipur
properties.source: Rajasthan Adhyan Class 11 Ch 1 (Public Awakening & Freedom Struggle) + standard historiography

facts: Sethi resigned Chomu Thikana service to found Vardhman Pathshala (Jaipur, 1907) — a Jain seminary that doubled as a revolutionary nursery.
Pupils included Pratap Singh Barhath (Shahpura), Vishnudutt Bhargava and Motichand — the core of the 1912 Delhi-Hardinge conspiracy.
Arrested 1914 on a false mahant-murder charge; released 1920 after 6 years and turned to Gandhian methods.

properties.name: Rao Gopal Singh — Kharwa Armed Revolution (1915)
properties.source: Rajasthan Adhyan Class 11 Ch 1 (Public Awakening & Freedom Struggle) + standard historiography

facts: Kharwa Thikanedar Rao Gopal Singh, in contact with Rash Behari Bose, planned an armed Rajasthan-wide rising on 21 Feb 1915 — one of the Ghadar-linked conspiracies.
The plan was betrayed; Rao Gopal put under house-arrest at Tadgarh, escaped, was re-arrested and jailed at Ajmer till 1920.
Kesari Singh Barhath wrote: "As Punjab is proud of Lala Lajpat Rai, Rajasthan is proud of Rao Gopal Singh Kharwa."

properties.name: Kalibai Bhil — Rastapal Martyrdom (19 Jun 1947)
properties.source: Rajasthan Adhyan Class 11 Ch 1 (Public Awakening & Freedom Struggle) + standard historiography

facts: A Bhil schoolgirl of Rastapal village in Dungarpur, martyred on 19 June 1947 while shielding her teacher Sengabhai from state police fire.
The Rastapal Satyagraha was part of the Bhogilal Pandya-led Dungarpur Praja Mandal campaign; her sacrifice became the movement's emotional pivot.
Kalibai Balika Cycle Yojana (2007) — Rajasthan's free-cycle scheme for tribal girls — is named after her.

properties.name: Swami Dayanand & Arya Samaj — Ajmer
properties.source: Rajasthan Adhyan Class 11 Ch 1 (Public Awakening & Freedom Struggle) + standard historiography

facts: Swami Dayanand founded the Arya Samaj at Ajmer; the head office and his samadhi (Rishi Udyan) are here.

His 2nd Rajasthan visit (1881-83) took him to Bharatpur, Jaipur, Chittor, Udaipur (where he wrote the Satyarth Prakash preface) and Jodhpur; died at Ajmer 30 Oct 1883 — believed poisoned by the courtesan Nanhijan of Jodhpur.
Arya Samaj's Rajasthan wing produced Master Adityendra, Chandkaran & Harbilas Sarda and the Nagari Pracharini Sabha of Beawar — the intellectual backbone of the Praja Mandal era.

properties.name: Rajputana Madhya Bharat Sabha, Ajmer (1919-20)
properties.leaders: Jamnalal Bajaj (chair); Vijay Singh Pathik, Arjun Lal Sethi, Kesari Singh Barhath, Rao Gopal Singh
properties.source: Rajasthan Adhyan Class 11 Ch 1 (Public Awakening & Freedom Struggle) + standard historiography

facts: First all-Rajputana political forum; brought Rajasthan's revolutionaries and Gandhians onto a single platform under Jamnalal Bajaj.
Its 1920 session at Ajmer coincided with the arrival of the Rajasthan Seva Sangh (from Wardha) — the mainspring of Prajamandal-era organising.
The Prince of Wales boycott at Ajmer (28 Nov 1921) — Rajasthan's first mass non-cooperation event — emerged from this network.

properties.name: Bhagat Movement (Vagad, 1921-29)
properties.source: Rajasthan Adhyan Class 11 Ch 1 (Public Awakening & Freedom Struggle) + standard historiography

facts: Govind Guru's Bhil uplift work after his release from jail — organised the "Bhagat" (devout) sub-community across Banswara-Dungarpur.
Non-violent programme: end drinking and animal sacrifice, girl schooling, Vaishnava vegetarianism; a white-flag emblem borrowed from Kabir-panthi tradition.
Bhogilal Pandya's "Vagad Seva Mandir" (1930s Dungarpur) grew directly out of this movement.

properties.name: Seth Damodardas Rathi — Beawar (1910s-1920s)
properties.source: Rajasthan Adhyan Class 11 Ch 1 (Public Awakening & Freedom Struggle) + standard historiography

facts: Rathi ran his Krishna Mill accounts entirely in Hindi from 1914 — a Swadeshi-Nagari precedent later cited across northern India.
Founded Nagari Pracharini Sabha, Beawar; petitioned the British Commissioner to switch Ajmer-Merwara court records to Nagari.
Financed the underground work of Vijay Singh Pathik, Kesari Singh Barhath, Rao Gopal Singh and Arjun Lal Sethi — the five who steered Rajasthan's revolutionary phase.

properties.name: Shekhawati Peasant Movement (1922-34)
properties.leaders: Sardar Harlal Singh, Chetram Jat, Pt. Tara Kesh
properties.source: Rajasthan Adhyan Class 11 Ch 1 (Public Awakening & Freedom Struggle) + standard historiography

facts: Jat-caste peasant struggle against Rao Raja Kalyan Singh of Sikar and other Shekhawati Thikanas; the Jaipur Prime Minister Mirza Ismail sent troops.
Kudan (Sikar) firing 25 Apr 1935: 4 killed, 250 injured — the Shekhawati equivalent of Neemuchana.
Produced women leaders — Kishori Devi and Utma Devi organised satyagrahis; the movement was joined by the Meo peasant movement in Alwar-Bharatpur.

properties.name: Rastapal Satyagraha, Dungarpur (1947)
properties.source: Rajasthan Adhyan Class 11 Ch 1 (Public Awakening & Freedom Struggle) + standard historiography

facts: School-opening satyagraha in Bhil villages of Dungarpur under Bhogilal Pandya; the police fired on a school procession at Rastapal on 19 Jun 1947.
Kalibai Bhil died shielding her teacher Sengabhai — the day is observed as Kalibai Martyrs' Day.
One of the last Praja Mandal actions before Independence and Rajasthan's integration.

properties.name: Quit India — Rampura Kotwali, Kota (14-16 Aug 1942)
properties.source: Rajasthan Adhyan Class 11 Ch 1 (Public Awakening & Freedom Struggle) + standard historiography

facts: Kota students locked the state police in barracks, took over Rampura Kotwali and hoisted the tricolour on 14 August 1942.
The public administered the city for about two weeks until the Maharao promised no reprisals; arrested workers were released.
Rajasthan's longest civilian control during Quit India — 14-16 days of citizen rule.
